class_schedule.topic: E-commerce Business Models and Digital Transformation
learning_objectives: Classify e-commerce business models: B2C, B2B, C2C, D2C, marketplace
Analyze digital transformation strategies in traditional industries
Evaluate platform economics and network effects

lecture_notes.introduction: E-commerce has evolved from simply selling products online to a complete reimagining of how businesses create, deliver, and capture value through digital technologies.
key_concepts: E-commerce models: B2C, B2B, C2C, D2C, subscription, freemium
Platform business models and two-sided markets
Network effects: direct and indirect
Digital transformation vs. digitization
Disintermediation and re-intermediation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Business model canvas analysis comparing Shopify (enabler), Amazon (marketplace), and Warby Parker (D2C)

review.value: Each additional user increases the value of the platform for all users; creates winner-take-most dynamics in platform markets
Direct-to-Consumer: brands sell directly to end customers, bypassing traditional retail, enabling better margins and customer data ownership

learning_objectives: Evaluate e-commerce platform options: SaaS, open-source, custom
Understand payment processing and checkout optimization
Analyze cloud infrastructure and scalability requirements
readings: Shopify vs. WooCommerce vs. Magento Comparison

lecture_notes.introduction: Technology choices have profound business implications. The right e-commerce platform, payment system, and infrastructure determine what you can sell, how fast you can grow, and how much it costs.
key_concepts: E-commerce platforms: Shopify, WooCommerce, Magento, custom builds
Payment gateways: Stripe, PayPal, Square; PCI compliance
Checkout optimization: guest checkout, one-click, cart recovery
Cloud infrastructure: AWS, GCP, Azure for scalability
APIs and headless commerce architecture
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up a Shopify store from scratch: product catalog, payment gateway, checkout flow, and basic analytics

review.value: Decoupling the front-end presentation layer from the back-end commerce engine via APIs; enables omnichannel experiences from one backend
Average 70% of online shopping carts are abandoned; recovery emails and checkout optimization can recapture 10-15% of lost sales

class_schedule.topic: User Experience Design and Conversion Optimization
learning_objectives: Apply UX design principles to e-commerce sites
Conduct conversion rate optimization using A/B testing
readings: Krug, Don't Make Me Think (key concepts)

lecture_notes.introduction: In e-commerce, your website is your storefront. User experience directly determines conversion rates, average order value, and customer retention.
key_concepts: UX principles: clarity, simplicity, consistency, feedback
Conversion funnel analysis: identify and fix drop-off points
A/B testing methodology: hypothesis, test, analyze, implement
Mobile commerce: thumb-friendly design, progressive web apps
Personalization engines and product recommendations
lecture_notes.demonstration: CRO exercise: analyzing a real e-commerce site's conversion funnel, identifying friction points, and designing A/B test hypotheses

review.value: Systematic process of increasing the percentage of visitors who complete a desired action; data-driven, not opinion-based
Comparing two versions of a page element to determine which performs better; requires statistical significance before making decisions

class_schedule.topic: E-commerce Operations: Fulfillment, Logistics, and Customer Service
learning_objectives: Design fulfillment strategies: in-house, 3PL, dropshipping, FBA
Build customer service systems that drive retention

lecture_notes.introduction: E-commerce success depends on what happens after the click. Fulfillment speed, shipping costs, and customer service quality determine whether one-time buyers become loyal customers.
key_concepts: Fulfillment models: self-fulfillment, 3PL, FBA, dropshipping
Inventory management for e-commerce: SKU proliferation challenges
Last-mile delivery optimization and customer expectations
Returns management: reverse logistics and restocking
Customer service: chatbots, help desks, social media support
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a fulfillment strategy for a growing D2C brand, comparing cost and service tradeoffs across models

review.value: 3PL (third-party logistics) offers flexibility and customization; FBA (Fulfillment by Amazon) provides Prime eligibility and Amazon's infrastructure but less brand control
Repeat customers are 5-7x cheaper to retain than new customers to acquire; fulfillment quality drives repeat purchases

class_schedule.topic: Digital Payments, Security, and Legal Compliance
learning_objectives: Evaluate digital payment methods and emerging payment technologies
Navigate legal requirements: privacy, accessibility, consumer protection

lecture_notes.introduction: Trust is the currency of e-commerce. Security breaches, payment fraud, and privacy violations can destroy a digital business overnight.
key_concepts: Payment methods: credit cards, digital wallets, BNPL, cryptocurrency
PCI DSS compliance requirements for payment processing
SSL/TLS encryption and secure checkout
Fraud prevention: AVS, CVV, 3D Secure, machine learning
Legal compliance: GDPR, CCPA, ADA accessibility, FTC endorsement rules
lecture_notes.demonstration: Security audit of an e-commerce site: checking SSL, payment processing compliance, privacy policy, and accessibility

review.value: Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard: 12 requirements for any business that handles credit card data, including encryption, access controls, and monitoring
Buy Now Pay Later (Klarna, Affirm, Afterpay) increases average order value by 20-30% but introduces credit risk considerations

learning_objectives: Build an e-commerce analytics framework with key metrics
Develop growth strategies: acquisition, retention, and monetization
Analyze emerging trends: social commerce, voice commerce, AI personalization

lecture_notes.introduction: Growing an e-commerce business requires mastering the metrics that matter and continuously optimizing the three growth levers: getting more customers, keeping them longer, and increasing their spending.
key_concepts: E-commerce KPIs: AOV, conversion rate, CAC, LTV, churn rate
Growth framework: acquisition x retention x monetization
Cohort analysis and customer segmentation
Social commerce: Instagram Shopping, TikTok Shop, live commerce
AI in e-commerce: personalization, demand forecasting, dynamic pricing
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building an e-commerce dashboard in Google Analytics: tracking revenue, conversion funnel, product performance, and customer cohorts

review.value: Grouping customers by acquisition date and tracking their behavior over time; reveals whether retention is improving or declining
Revenue and cost per customer: if LTV > CAC, the business model is viable; if not, growth accelerates losses

questions.question: A traditional brick-and-mortar furniture retailer wants to launch an e-commerce channel. Should they build on Shopify, WooCommerce, or a custom platform? Justify your recommendation considering their specific industry challenges.
questions.answer: Shopify Plus is the best recommendation for a traditional retailer entering e-commerce. Reasons: (1) Fast time-to-market with minimal technical expertise needed, critical for a retailer without e-commerce experience. (2) Built-in payment processing and security (PCI compliance handled). (3) Furniture-specific challenges are addressed: Shopify supports high-resolution product imagery, AR product visualization apps, configurable products, and complex shipping rate calculations for large items. (4) Scalability handled by Shopify's infrastructure. WooCommerce requires more technical management; custom build is too expensive and slow for a first e-commerce venture. As they grow, they can migrate if needed.

rubric.key: Explains why alternatives are less suitable

questions.question: An e-commerce site has a 2.5% conversion rate on desktop but only 0.8% on mobile, despite 65% of traffic coming from mobile. Identify two likely causes and propose specific solutions.
questions.answer: Cause 1: Poor mobile checkout experience. Mobile users likely encounter friction: small form fields, no autofill, no digital wallet options. Solution: Implement Apple Pay and Google Pay for one-tap checkout, enable autofill, and reduce form fields to minimum required. Cause 2: Slow mobile page load times. Product images and scripts optimized for desktop create slow loads on mobile connections. Solution: Implement lazy loading for images, use WebP format, enable AMP or build as a Progressive Web App, and target under 3-second load time (53% of mobile users abandon sites that take over 3 seconds).

rubric.key: Specific solutions proposed for each cause

questions.question: A small e-commerce brand selling handmade candles (200 orders/month) is considering switching from self-fulfillment to a 3PL. What factors should they evaluate, and at what order volume does outsourcing typically make sense?
questions.answer: Factors to evaluate: (1) Cost comparison: current per-order fulfillment cost (labor, packing materials, shipping rates) vs. 3PL fees (storage, pick-and-pack, shipping). (2) Time: hours spent on fulfillment vs. hours that could be spent on marketing and product development. (3) Scalability: can self-fulfillment handle holiday spikes or viral growth? (4) Shipping speed and cost: 3PLs often have better carrier rates and multiple warehouse locations for faster delivery. (5) Product handling: candles are fragile and may require special packaging. General rule: outsourcing becomes cost-effective at 100-300 orders/month for most products. At 200 orders, they are at the threshold. If growth trajectory suggests 500+ orders within 6 months, start the 3PL transition now.

questions.question: What are the minimum PCI DSS requirements a small e-commerce merchant must meet if they use Stripe or Shopify Payments to process credit cards?
questions.answer: When using Stripe or Shopify Payments, the merchant never directly handles credit card data (it goes directly to the payment processor's servers). This reduces PCI scope significantly. Minimum requirements: (1) Complete the PCI DSS Self-Assessment Questionnaire A (SAQ-A) annually, which is the simplest form. (2) Ensure the website uses TLS/SSL encryption (HTTPS) for all pages. (3) Do not store, process, or transmit cardholder data on their own servers. (4) Maintain secure passwords and restrict access to payment settings. (5) Ensure third-party scripts on the site are monitored for tampering. The payment processor (Stripe/Shopify) handles the heavy PCI compliance burden as a Level 1 service provider.

rubric.key: Understands reduced scope with hosted payment processing

major_assignments.title: E-commerce Business Plan and Prototype
major_assignments.description: Develop a comprehensive e-commerce business plan for a new online venture, including business model, technology platform selection, UX wireframes, marketing strategy, operations plan, and financial projections. Build a functional prototype on Shopify or equivalent platform.
objectives: Design a viable e-commerce business model
Select and justify technology platform and infrastructure decisions
Create UX wireframes and a functional store prototype
Develop financial projections including unit economics
deliverables: Functional Shopify prototype store with 10+ products
UX wireframes for key user flows

criteria: Clear value proposition and target market
Revenue model and pricing strategy defined
Functional store with realistic product catalog
Unit economics clearly defined (CAC, LTV, AOV)
Viable business model with clear differentiation
Sound financial projections with unit economics

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of e-commerce concepts and technology

hands_on_labs.description: Practical exercises building and optimizing e-commerce experiences

major_assignments.description: E-commerce business plan and prototype project

midterm_examination.description: Covers e-commerce models, technology, UX, and operations

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including analytics, growth strategies, and emerging trends

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance required. Lab sessions involve hands-on platform work that builds cumulatively.
course_policies.late_submissions: Business plan milestones penalized 10% per day. Prototype must be live by deadline for evaluation.
course_policies.collaboration: Business plan and prototype may be completed in teams of 2-3. Quizzes and exams are individual.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Prototype stores must be original work. Using pre-built Shopify themes is allowed but must be customized significantly.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Shopify partner account (free). Domain name for prototype. Google Analytics access.
